segments.s697-g97: چو here is the conjunction 'when' (an archaic/poetic usage) attached to the following name/phrase; بر زین بیامد is a simple past clause: بر = onto, زین = saddle, بیامد = 'mounted/came onto'. The final برش is the enclitic -ش (him/his) attached to بر in the manuscript tokenization; syntactically it marks the person toward whom the ensuing look is directed (i.e. 'toward him').
segments.s697-g98: نگه کرد is a light verb construction: نگه (gaze/look) + کرد (did/made) = 'he looked/fixed his gaze'. The following coordinated list 'روی و سر و افسرش' gives the items examined: روی = face, سر = head, افسرش = افسر (helmet) with the possessive/target suffix -ش 'his/him'. The conjunctions و simply coordinate the nouns.
segments.s697-g99: همی‌بود is an imperfective/past-continuous habitual form (همی- prefixed for continuous aspect + بود 'was/continued to be') meaning 'remained' or 'kept being'. پیشش = 'before her/in her presence' (پیش + enclitic -ش). زمانی دراز is a temporal adverbial 'for a long time' (زمانی 'for a while/time' + دراز 'long').
segments.s697-g100: This is a noun phrase + verb: طلسم فریبنده = 'a deceptive/enchanting spell' (طلسم [spell/enchantment] modified by فریبنده 'deceptive'). بردش here functions as a past verb with enclitic -ش = 'it/it-to-him carried/took him/away from him' (older usage of برد 'to carry/take'); نماز is the affected noun 'prayer/devotion'. Word order is compacted in poetry, so the verb sits between adjective phrase and object.
segments.s697-g101: بسی گفت is an idiomatic short clause: بسی = 'much' (archaic/adverbial) + گفت (said/spoke) — 'he spoke much.' The coordinated clause 'و زن هیچ پاسخ نداد' = 'and the woman gave no reply': هیچ negates 'پاسخ' (no answer) and نداد is the simple past negative 'did not give'.
segments.s697-g102: پراندیشه is an adjective/epithet 'pensive/thoughtful' (literary compound). شد is the past of 'become' = 'became'. مرد مهتر نژاد is a nominal phrase 'the man, of chief/distinguished lineage' where مهتر نژاد qualifies مرد (postnominal style is common in older Persian).
segments.s697-g103: سراپای زن is a fixed expression 'head to foot/the whole body of the woman' (سراپا = entirely, زن = woman). The token راهمی‌بنگرید reflects fused elements: را (object marker) + هم (also/emphatic) + می‌بنگرید (old present/imperfect form of 'look'); together it yields 'he looked her all over' — note poetic orthography fuses these into one word here.
segments.s697-g104: پرستندگان را is the direct object marked by را ('the worshippers'). بر او is a locative/relational phrase 'on/around her' (بر = on/upon/toward + او 'her'). بدید is the past form 'saw' (in older Persian spelling; here third-person past). The whole clause: 'he saw the worshippers upon/around her.'
segments.s697-g49: دلاور (subject) + نخست (adverb) set the order 'the brave man first'; اندر آمد is a past tense verbal phrase 'came in/entered'; بپند is an infinitival/performative verbal complement 'to ponder/consider' (here following آمد, so 'came in to think'). The construction is literal: subject — time adverb — motion verb — purpose/infinitive.
segments.s697-g50: سخنها 'the words/sayings' is the head of a relative/clausal comment introduced by که 'that/which'; او را is a dative/benefactive phrase 'to him' (را is the object/marker placed after the pronoun); بدی is the noun 'evil/harm' and سودمند is a predicate adjective 'useful/advantageous', so the clause reads 'that evil was useful to him' (i.e. 'he thought wrongdoing would benefit him').
segments.s697-g51: بدو + گفت: بدو here is the prepositional/dative pronoun 'to him/unto him' (not the imperative 'run!'), placed before گفت (said) to indicate the recipient. کای is a vocative 'O', and دخت قیصر نژاد is a genitive phrase 'daughter of Caesar's line' (دخت = daughter/maiden; قیصر نژاد = of Caesaric descent). Note: گفت is being used as past-tense 'said'.
segments.s697-g52: خردمند functions as a substantive/adjective 'the wise' or 'wise one'; نخروشد is an archaic/poetic negative jussive/subjunctive form (here expressing a wish or negation — 'may not .../let not ... occur') built with ن-; از کار داد is a prepositional phrase 'from the affair/matter of داد (justice)', so the whole clause reads roughly 'may the wise not be removed/estranged from matters of justice.' Word order places the negative verb-form before the prepositional complement.
segments.s697-g53: رهانیست is a fused predicate meaning 'there is (no) release/escape' (here with the sense 'no escape'); از is the preposition 'from'; مرگ 'death' is the object of از; پران is an adjective 'winged/feathered' modifying عقاب 'eagle'. The sequence expresses inability to escape the eagle's death: 'there is no escape from death (caused by) the winged eagle.'
segments.s697-g54: چه ... و چه ... is a correlative pair meaning 'whether ... or ...'; در بیشه شیر 'the lion in the thicket' and ماهی در آب 'the fish in the water' are parallel examples. Each internal word follows normal prepositional order (در + place + creature). The clause gives two instances to show the eagle's reach.
segments.s697-g55: همه 'everyone' is the subject/agent; باد here is the optative particle meaning 'may/let' (a wish or curse); بد گفتن is a compound verbal expression 'to speak ill' (بد modifying گفتن), and پهلوان is the object/topic 'the champion' (بد گفتن پهلوان = to speak ill of the champion). The construction is optative: 'May all speak ill of the champion.'
segments.s697-g56: که here gives a causal/explanatory clause 'for/because'; زن 'the woman' is the subject, بی‌زبان is a compound adjective 'without tongue' = 'mute', بود is the past copula 'was', و is 'and', تن 'the body/person', بی‌روان 'without spirit' = 'lifeless'. Grammatically it is a simple past descriptive clause explaining why people should speak ill.
segments.s697-g1: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when' introducing a temporal clause. خورشید گردنده is a noun (خورشید) modified by a participial adjective (گردنده, ‘circling/revolving’) placed after the noun. بی‌رنگ شد is the past-tense predicate ('became pale/colorless'); شد is the past form of شدن indicating change of state.
segments.s697-g2: ستاره is the subject 'the star'. به برج شباهنگ شد is a predicative phrase where به marks movement/entry into (here 'into/unto' a constellation or sign), برج is used in the sense of a zodiac/constellation, شباهنگ is an epithet/name ('nightingale') applied to that constellation, and شد is the past 'became/entered'.
segments.s697-g3: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb here meaning 'he commanded/ordered/said'. قیصر is the subject 'Caesar' (the ruler). به introduces the indirect object/dative 'to' (here 'to the trick-maker'), نیرنگ ساز is a compound meaning 'maker of tricks' or 'deceiver/schemer' (نیرنگ = stratagem, ساز here as the means/maker element). Note the verb precedes the subject for poetic order.
segments.s697-g4: که introduces the content/purpose clause ('that' / 'so that'). پیش آرد is a verbal phrase meaning 'bring forward' / 'produce' (آرد = 'bring' here; preceded by پیش 'before/forth'). آرد is the present/subjunctive form used in a subordinate clause after a verb of command. اندیشه‌های دراز is a noun phrase 'long/long-ranging thoughts' i.e. 'far‑reaching schemes/plans' with دراز qualifying اندیشه‌ها.
segments.s697-g5: بسازید is an imperative (or polite plural) 'make/construct' directed to the addressee. جای شگفتی is a collocation meaning 'a thing/place of wonder' (i.e. 'a marvel'). طلسم is the object: 'talisman' or 'enchantment'—the whole phrase commands making 'a wondrous talisman.'
segments.s697-g6: که introduces the result/purpose clause 'so that'. کس combined with a negative verb here functions as 'no one' (sense 1). بازنشناسد is a negative present/subjunctive-form meaning 'does not/will not recognize' (used here as desired result). او را is the definite object 'him/her' with را marking it, and به جسم uses به in the instrumental/locative sense 'by/through his body' (i.e. 'recognize him by his physical form').
segments.s697-g7: نشسته is a past participle 'seated' placed before the noun to describe state. زنی خوب is 'a woman, beautiful/fair' (زنی = a woman; خوب = 'beautiful' here). برتخت is a fused prepositional phrase 'on the throne/couch' (بر + تخت). ناز qualifies the throne (a poetic word meaning 'coquetry/delicacy/softness'), giving the sense 'a delicate/dainty throne'.
segments.s697-g8: پراز is 'full of' and governs the following noun شرم ('shame'/'modesty') to yield 'full of modesty/shyness'. با marks accompaniment 'with' and introduces جامه‌های طراز: جامه‌های is the plural 'garments/robes' and طراز describes them as 'of fine fashion/pattern' (finely fashioned). Together this describes her as modestly adorned in elegant clothes.
segments.s697-g33: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. او is the third-person subject. نشستست is an older/poetic perfective/stative form meaning 'is seated/has sat' (subject + verb order is normal in classical Persian). با + سوک و درد is a prepositional phrase 'with mourning and sorrow' where سوک is a poetic noun for mourning and درد names the emotional pain; the conjunction و simply connects the two nouns.
segments.s697-g34: شده is the past participle/auxiliary meaning 'has become.' روز روشن is a noun + adjective 'a bright day.' برو لاژورد is a compact poetic phrase giving the quality of that brightness (لاژورد = lapis-lazuli). برو here is a poetic/decorative linking particle whose exact modern equivalent isn't provided in the lexical senses given, so its pragmatic role is to associate روشن with لاژورد (i.e. 'bright like/with lapis-lazuli').
segments.s697-g35: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. پندم is 'my counsel' (پند + enclitic -م 'my') functioning as the object of پذیرد. پذیرد is a present/factual third-person singular verb 'accept' (classical present). گوید here is the verb 'speak' (used with سخن after it); the word order 'نه گوید سخن' places the verb before its object, a common poetic/inverted order in classical Persian.
segments.s697-g36: جهان نو is noun + adjective 'the new world.' از رنج او is a prepositional phrase expressing cause ('because of his suffering' / 'from his suffering'). شد is the past-tense verb 'became' and کهن is the predicate adjective 'old/ancient' describing the result. The clause literally reads 'the new world, because of his suffering, became old.'
segments.s697-g37: یکی here is an indefinite vocative 'someone' or 'one (do this)'. رنج بردار is an imperative construction: رنج (suffering) + بردار (imperative 'take/bear'), addressed to an unspecified person. و connects clauses. او راببین is object-marked: او + را (object marker) + ببین/ببین (imperative 'see' / 'look at') — the object precedes the verb in this poetic compression.
segments.s697-g38: سخنهای دانندگان is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the words/utterances of the wise/experts' (سخنهای = words/speeches, دانندگان = knowers/experts). برگزین is an imperative 'choose/select' directed at the addressee; the object is the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s697-g39: جوانی و از گوهر پهلوان lists two related topics: 'youth' and 'from the hero's essence' (i.e. 'the hero's mettle/quality'). از introduces the genitive/partitive relation 'of/from,' so گوهر پهلوان reads as 'the essence/quality of the champion.' This is a compact, poetic nominal phrase rather than a full clause.
segments.s697-g40: مگر here works as a conditional 'unless.' با تو places the interlocutor in company 'with you.' او is the third-person subject. برگشاید is a present/subjunctive or optative third-person form meaning 'opens/should open' (زبان برگشاید = 'his tongue opens' = 'he speaks'). زبان is the object 'tongue' used idiomatically for 'speech/utterance.' The phrase thus expresses 'unless he opens his tongue with you' (i.e. speaks to you).
segments.s697-g121: طلسمست is a poetic contraction of طلسم است (“it is a talisman”), where the copula is fused to the noun. کاین introduces a relative clause (‘that/which these…’); رومیان is the plural subject of the following verb; ساختند is simple past, 3rd-person plural (‘they made’). Together this segment states the nominal claim and introduces who made it.
segments.s697-g122: که here continues the relative clause (‘by/which’ or ‘such that’). بالوی و گستهم are two proper names coordinated with و (the object(s) of recognition), placed before the verb in typical Persian order. نشناختند is past negative, 3rd-person plural (‘they did not know/recognize’), with its subject (رومیان) understood from the previous clause.
segments.s697-g123: بایرانیان is an archaic/prepositional-like form meaning ‘to/at the Iranians’ (acting as the indirect object). بربخندی is a gerundial/verb phrase ‘by smiling / if you smile at…’ with the prefix بر- marking direction (‘upon/at’) and the -ی ending on the verb form; همی is a classical present/emphatic particle indicating ongoing or general action. The whole segment gives the first conditional/alternative action.
segments.s697-g124: وگر is the conditional conjunction ‘and if / but if’. چشم ما را is the direct object phrase (‘our eyes’) with را marking definiteness/object. ببندی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive or optative (‘you close / you were to close’) and here forms the conditional counterpart to the first clause; همی again gives the classical present/emphatic flavor. The subject (‘you’) is implied in the verb endings.
segments.s697-g125: چواین is the fused temporal expression چو + این meaning ‘when this (happens / is heard)’. بشنود is the present/subjunctive form used after چو to mark the time (‘he hears’); شاه is the logical subject placed after the temporal clause (inversion typical of Persian poetry). خندان شود is a predicate with خندان (smiling) as adjective and شود (‘becomes’) as the copular/auxiliary verb: ‘he becomes smiling’ → ‘he smiles / grows merry’.
segments.s697-g126: This coordinate clause predicates two qualities of the king. گشاده رخ is a compound adjective phrase ‘open-faced / with a broad smile’; و connects the next predicate; سیم دندان is a descriptive compound literally ‘silver-toothed’ (i.e. gleaming teeth). شود repeats the copular verb ‘becomes’ and applies to both predicates, yielding ‘his face brightens and his teeth become like silver.’
segments.s697-g41: بدو گفت = 'he said to him': بدو is the indirect object ('to him') placed before the verb گفت (past tense 'said'), a common poetic order. گستهم here follows and functions as the addressee/name or an inserted word; کایدون is a vocative ('O schemer' / 'O treacherous one') and کنم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive form of کردن used in a rhetorical question ('what shall I do' / 'should I do').
segments.s697-g42: مگر introduces a negative/rhetorical clause here ('except' / 'unless' / 'what else'), followed by a prepositional phrase از دلش ('from his heart') where ش is the enclitic genitive 'his'. رنج بیرون کنم places the noun rɒnj 'sorrow/pain' before the compound verb بیرون کردن + کنم ('to put/drive out' + 1st-person subjunctive), so literally 'to drive the pain out I (will) do' = 'to drive the pain out of his heart.'
segments.s697-g43: بنزد طلسم آمد — the locative/goal بنزد 'to/near the presence of' precedes its object طلسم, then the verb آمد ('came'); آن نامدار ('that renowned one') appears after the verb by poetic inversion and is the grammatical subject. This order (preposition + object, verb, then subject) is typical in epic verse.
segments.s697-g44: گشاده دل is an adjective phrase meaning 'open‑hearted' (گشاده modifying دل). و simply connects phrases. بر سخن کامگار is a prepositional phrase where بر 'toward/on' governs سخن 'speech' and کامگار ('successful/satisfied' / 'fulfilled') functions as an adjective describing disposition toward speaking — i.e. 'ready/eager for speech.' The adjective follows the noun, a normal poetic order.
segments.s697-g45: چوآمد is a fused temporal clause 'when he came' (چو + آمد). به نزدیک marks the approach ('to/near'), and تختش فراز is a genitival phrase 'his high/lofty throne' where تختش ('his throne') is followed by the attributive فراز ('high, lofty'), reflecting poetic inversion of modifiers.
segments.s697-g46: This clause has طلسم as the subject. از بر تخت is a doubled locative ('from upon the throne' — از + بر + تخت) and بردش is the past verb برد ('carried/took away') with the enclitic object -ش ('it/him'). نماز is the direct object 'prayer.' The poetic ordering places the PP between subject and verb; the sequence yields 'the talisman, from upon the throne, took it — the prayer.'
segments.s697-g47: گرانمایه is an epithet 'noble/precious' placed before the name گستهم (here the proper name) — a common epic epithet + name sequence. بنشست is the past tense 'sat down' and خوار is a predicative adjective 'abased/humbled' describing his posture or state; the order Adjective + Name + Verb + Predicative is poetic inversion.
segments.s697-g48: سخن گفت is a light-verb construction 'spoke' (literally 'said words'). با is the preposition 'with' and دختر سوکوار is a noun phrase 'the daughter/maiden of the archer' (دختر 'maiden/daughter' modified by the epithet سوکوار 'archer/marksman'). Word order is verb then the with-phrase.
segments.s697-g89: ازان نامداران is a partitive/genitive phrase meaning 'of those renowned (men)' or 'among those famous ones' (ازان = 'of those/thereupon'); چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; چاره نیافت is a negative past-tense verbal clause (چاره = 'remedy/solution' as the object placed before the verb, نیافت = past of یافتن, 'he found not'). The whole segment = 'when he found no remedy among those renowned.'
segments.s697-g90: سوی رای خراد is a directional phrase: سوی = 'toward'; رای = 'counsel/opinion' and خراد qualifies it ('of wisdom' or 'wise') so together 'toward the counsel of wisdom' (خراد here treated as the abstract 'wisdom'). بر زین شتافت is a motion clause where the prepositional phrase 'بر زین' ('onto the saddle') precedes the past verb شتافت ('he hastened/ran'), describing a quick mounting action. The verb شتافت is a simple past used to express sudden movement.
segments.s697-g91: بدو گفت is literally 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', گفت = past of گفتن 'said'); کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; نامدار دبیر is an epithet 'renowned scribe' with the adjective/epithet (نامدار) preceding the noun (دبیر). The sequence marks direct speech introduced to the scribe.
segments.s697-g92: گزین is an imperative 'Choose' (imperative form addressed to the addressee). سر تخمه اردشیر is a noun phrase: سر here means 'chief/head' (leader) and governs تخمه اردشیر ('the seed/offspring of Ardashir' — تخمه = 'offspring/stock' with اردشیر as its possessor). Together: 'Choose the head/chief of Ardashir's lineage.'
segments.s697-g93: یکی is an indefinite pronoun 'someone/a certain one'; سوی این دختر is the directional phrase 'to this girl/maiden' (سوی = 'toward', این دختر = 'this maiden'); اندر شوی is a present-subjunctive/jussive clause built on شدن ('شوی') used imperatively/jussively here: literally 'let (him) go in' or 'go/enter'. The whole segment functions as the command 'Let someone go to this maiden.'
segments.s697-g94: مگر introduces a purposive/conditional nuance here (often rendered 'so that' / 'in order that' in older Persian); یک ره is an adverbial phrase 'one time/once' (ره used adverbially); آواز او is 'her voice' (آواز = 'voice', او = 'she/her'); بشنوی is the present-subjunctive (or jussive) of شنیدن 'you may hear' used to express the desired/purposed outcome. Together: 'so that you may hear her voice once.'
segments.s697-g95: فرستاد is the past-tense transitive verb 'he sent'; با او here marks accompaniment or the person 'with whom' the sender sent someone (literally 'he sent, with him, one stalwart'); یکی استوار is the noun phrase 'a stalwart/steadfast man' (یکی = 'a certain one', استوار used as noun/adjective 'stalwart'). The word order places the sent person after the accompaniment phrase, a common poetic/order-flexible arrangement.
segments.s697-g96: ز ایوان indicates origin 'from the iwan' (ز = 'from', ایوان = 'iwan/portico'); به نزدیک آن سوکوار gives the destination/direction 'to the vicinity of that archer' (به = 'to/toward', نزدیک = 'near', آن = 'that', سوکوار = 'archer'). Together they form a from–to phrase describing where the stalwart was sent.
segments.s697-g57: به انگشت خود هر زمانی سرشک is a prepositional/temporal phrase: به + noun expresses direction/place here ('onto' or 'on'); انگشت خود = 'her own finger' (noun + reflexive خود in postnominal position); هر زمانی is an adverbial phrase 'at every time / at each moment'; سرشک is the noun 'a teardrop' that is the thing being let fall. Word order is typical classical Persian: prepositional phrase + temporal adverb + noun.
segments.s697-g58: بینداختی پیش گویا پزشک contains the verb بینداختی (past action 'let fall / dropped'). پیش here means 'in front of / before / in the presence of'. گویا modifies پزشک and is used adjectivally as 'eloquent / speaking' (an epithet for the physician). The clause therefore states the action (she let the tear fall) and locates it with respect to the physician.
segments.s697-g59: چوگستهم ازو در شگفتی بماند is a temporal clause introduced by چو = 'when'. چوگستهم is a fused, somewhat archaic past form meaning 'when (he) became / was' (classical narrative usage). ازو = 'by/at/from her' marks the cause or object of astonishment. در شگفتی بماند = verb phrase 'remained in astonishment' (بماند here denotes continued state). Together: 'When he became astonished at her, he stayed amazed.'
segments.s697-g60: فرستاد قیصر کس او را بخواند has verb–subject order common in epic style: فرستاد (sent) then قیصر (Caesar) as the agent. کس = 'someone' (an indefinite person) is the direct object of فرستاد, and او را بخواند is a purpose/infinitival clause ('to call/summon him/her') where بخواند is a subjunctive/infinitival-use of خواندن meaning 'to call/summon'. Literally: 'Caesar sent someone to call her.'
segments.s697-g61: چه دیدی بدوگفت is a reported-question structure: چه = 'what' (interrogative); دیدی = past 'did you see' (reported as speech); بدو گفت = 'he said to him'. The interrogative clause (چه دیدی) precedes the reporting verb, so this is 'He said to him, "What did you see..."'. از دخترم that follows specifies the topic of the question ('about my daughter').
segments.s697-g62: کزو تیره گردد همی افسرم is a subordinate clause introduced by کزو ('from which / from whom' = چه + از or 'that from whom'), marking the agent/source: 'from whom'. تیره گردد = 'becomes dark' (گردد is classical present/subjunctive/predicate meaning 'become' used here with future/optative sense); همی is an archaic emphatic particle ('indeed' / 'thus'); افسرم = 'my afsar/crown/helmet' (possessive suffix -م). The sense: 'that from whom my crown will be darkened.'
segments.s697-g63: بدو گفت بسیار دادمش پند = 'He said to him: I gave her much advice.' بدو = 'to him'; گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here introducing what follows); بسیار = 'much / many' (quantifier modifying پند); دادمش is the past compound 'I gave it/her' (دادم + enclitic -ش referring back to the advice or to the person), so the whole means 'I gave him/her much advice.'
segments.s697-g64: نبد پند من پیش او کاربند is a negative-result statement. نبد is an archaic negative/past copular 'was not / did not exist'; پند من = 'my advice'; پیش او = 'before him/in his case / with respect to her' (= 'on her'); کاربند here means 'to take effect / to be operative' (classical adjective/participle). Together: 'my advice was not effective on her' (i.e., 'my counsel did not work').
segments.s697-g105: همی‌گفت‌گر is a progressive/reporting verbal form meaning “kept saying” (continuous past narration). The clause that follows is a copular clause: زن زغم بیهش است = “the woman is (in) sorrow for him,” where زغم (’from sorrow’) governs بیهش (an archaic/clitic form meaning ‘for him’). The final است is the present copula linking the predicate to زن.
segments.s697-g106: پرستنده is the subject ‘the worshipper/lover/admirer’; باری here is an adverb meaning ‘at times/now and then.’ چرا خامش است is a direct question/remark ‘why is he silent?’ (خامش is a poetic form meaning ‘silent’). The clause order is subject + adverb + question phrase + copula.
segments.s697-g107: اگر introduces a conditional: اگر خود سرشکست در چشم اوی = ‘if he himself is humiliated/in disgrace in her eyes.’ خود is the emphatic/reflexive ‘himself’; سرشکست is an adjectival/predicative participle ‘humiliated/overthrown’; در چشم اوی is a fixed idiom ‘in the eye/gaze of her (i.e. in her regard).’
segments.s697-g108: سزیدی here functions as an evaluative predicate meaning roughly ‘it would be fitting/just’ (classical usage related to ‘deserve’). The following conditional اگر کم شدی خشم اوی = ‘if his anger were to lessen’ (کم شدی = ‘become less/diminished’), with خشم اوی ‘his/her anger.’ The whole phrase expresses that a reduction of anger would be appropriate in that case.
segments.s697-g109: به پیش marks direction/toward the presence (‘before/ in front of’); برش here is an object/locative element (archaic/poetic clitic) indicating ‘upon him/her/it’ (the supplied senses list does not include this clitic use, so the precise dictionary senses don’t match). بر + چکاند = ‘to cause to drip/fall upon’; همی shows continuous aspect ‘kept dripping/letting fall.’ The clause means he repeatedly let something (a tear) fall in front of her.
segments.s697-g110: چپ و راست is a coordinate phrase ‘left and right’ (i.e. sideways); جنبش نداند همی uses نداند in the negative-sense ‘does not know how / cannot’ so the idiom means ‘he could not move (to) left or right.’ همی again gives ongoing aspect. The whole clause describes inability to stir in either direction.
segments.s697-g111: سرشکش is a diminutive ‘little teardrop’; که introduces a relative/time clause ‘which/when’; انداخت is the verbal root ‘cast/threw/fell’ used here to mean ‘fell’; یک جای رفت = ‘went to one place/settled in one spot.’ The clause narrates the single drop’s fall and where it went.
segments.s697-g112: This is a paired negative formula: نه ... ونه = ‘neither ... nor ...’. جنبـان شدش دست = ‘his hand did not stir/move’ (جنبان ‘moving’, شدش = ‘became/occurred to him’—here forming the verbal idea ‘moved’); ونه پای رفت = ‘nor did his foot go/move.’ The verbs are simple stative/perfective forms used to report lack of motion.
segments.s697-g65: دگر روز is a temporal phrase where دگر (literally “other/another”) precedes روز to mean “the next day.” قیصر is the subject. به + بالوی marks the indirect object (“to Baluy”), and گفت is the past-tense verb “said” placed at the end as is normal in classical Persian word order (Subject — indirect object — Verb).
segments.s697-g66: که introduces reported speech (“that/that…”) and begins the content of what was said. امروز is the time adverb “today.” با + اندیان gives the accompaniment/partner (“with Andiyan”); باش is the imperative/subjunctive of بودن used here as a command “be,” and جفت is the predicate noun/complement “paired/partner,” so باش جفت = “be a pair / pair up.”
segments.s697-g67: همان نیز is an emphatic/connecting phrase: همان “that/the same” and نیز “also/likewise,” linking this action to the previous one. شاپور is the personal name; مهتر نژاد is a two-word epithet meaning “chief/foremost of lineage” (an attributive phrase describing Shapur’s high birth).
segments.s697-g68: کند is the finite verb “makes/does” (here in present/habitual or narrative present sense). جان ما is the direct object meaning “our life/heart” (i.e., our feelings). رابدین دخت is a noun phrase naming the maiden (the giver/thing involved) and follows the object; شاد is a predicative adjective “happy/glad,” functioning as the resultative complement of کند (i.e., “made our hearts glad [by Râbedin the daughter]”).
segments.s697-g69: شوی is the (optative/imperative) form of شدن meaning “become” or an instruction “be.” پیش + این دختر places the action spatially “in the presence of / before this maiden.” سوکوار is the predicate noun “marksman/archer,” so the whole clause is an instruction: “Be a marksman before this maiden.”
segments.s697-g70: سخن‌گویی is a verbal noun meaning “speaking” or “address” used here as an imperative-like instruction “speak.” ازنامور شهریار is a genitival/attributive phrase: ازنامور “illustrious/of famed renown” modifies شهریار “sovereign/king,” so the instruction is to speak concerning or in praise of the famed sovereign.
segments.s697-g71: مگر here functions as a conditional conjunction “unless.” پاسخی is the indefinite direct object “an answer/a reply.” یابی is the 2nd-person singular subjunctive/optative of یابیدن used to express the hoped-for or potential action “you may obtain / you get.” از دخترم marks the source “from my daughter.”
segments.s697-g72: کزو is a relative adverb “from which/from whom” linking to پاسخی. آتش آید is a clause with آتش “fire” as subject and آید (present/subjunctive of آمدن) “comes/would come”; together they express a metaphorical calamity “fire comes.” همی is an archaic/poetic particle used in classical Persian to mark continuous or emphatic aspect (here underscoring the action: “would surely come”). برسرم is the prepositional phrase “upon my head,” an idiom for suffering trouble or shame.
segments.s697-g73: مگر here introduces a possibility/rhetorical guess ('perhaps'/'is it that...'). بشنود is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive form of شنیدن used to express a possible or prospective action ('may/hear/will hear'). پند و اندرزتان is the direct object: two nouns joined by و (and) with the possessive suffix -تان ('your advice and counsel'). Word order is verb after preverb + object, typical of Persian clauses with modal flavor.
segments.s697-g74: بداند is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of دانستن here meaning 'come to know' or 'realize'. سرماهی is a noun meaning 'capital/principal/standing' and وارزتان is a compound with و- + ارز + -تان meaning 'and your worth/esteem' (possessive -تان). The clause states the result: 'she will know/recognize your capital and worth.'
segments.s697-g75: برآنم is a fixed expression 'I am resolved/intent' (بر + آن + ـم). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. امروز is a temporal adverb 'today'. پاسخ دهد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive (دهد) with پاسخ as object and expresses the intended action 'will give an answer / will answer.'
segments.s697-g76: چوپاسخ is 'چو + پاسخ' meaning 'when/as an answer' (introducing the manner/time of the reply). به here functions as an instrument/marker 'with/by' (i.e. 'with a voice of...'). آواز فرخ is a noun phrase 'a joyful/fortunate voice' (آواز = voice; فرخ = glad/fortunate); دهد is again 'gives'—together: 'when she replies, she will give it with a joyful voice.'
segments.s697-g77: شود is the 3rd-person singular present/future auxiliary 'becomes' or 'will be'. رسته is a past-participle/adjective meaning 'freed' or 'released' (resultative). زین here precedes انده; in classical usage زِ + این can mean 'from this', but the literal lexemes given in the word list do not match that usage (see senses). سوکوار is the noun 'archer' and is the patient/theme being freed: 'the archer will be freed from this sorrow.'
segments.s697-g78: که here introduces a result/cause clause ('so that' / 'for that reason'). خوناب is a compound noun 'blood-water' (i.e. a bloody stream). بارد is used as a present verb 'falls/pours' (classical form of باریدن), while همی is the continuous/formal present marker emphasizing ongoing action. برکنار in context means 'on the bank/side' (literally 'upon the edge'), though the senses in the provided list do not include the exact locative sense.
segments.s697-g79: برفت is a past-tense compound verb 'bar + raft' meaning 'went away / departed' (3rd person singular past). آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'. گرامی is an adjective 'honored/esteemed'. سه آزادمرد is a numeral + noun 'three free/noble men' forming the subject: 'those three honored noblemen departed.'
segments.s697-g80: سخن گوی is a compound meaning 'one who speaks' or 'a spokesman' (سخن = speech, گوی here forms the agent 'speaker'). وهریک means 'and each one'. بننگ is a noun 'shame/disgrace' (with the prefix ب- here part of the word). نبرد in this clause functions as a negative-polarity verb phrase ('did not bear/suffer') historically formed from بردن; the meanings listed in the word-data do not match the verbal use, so the sense index is not provided for that entry.
segments.s697-g9: This phrase lists directions of approach. ازین (az + īn) is a poetic compound meaning “from this” (از + این); روی here means “side/face” (not literally the person’s visage but the side/way). زان رو repeats the pattern for “from that side.” پرستندگان is the plural noun “worshippers” (those who revere or adore) placed after the locational phrase.
segments.s697-g10: پس is a connective adverb here (“then/thereupon”). پشت و پیش is the paired spatial phrase “behind and before/in front,” with پشت = rear and پیش = in front/in the presence of. اندرش is the older/pre-classical form meaning “in/around him,” where -ش = his/it attaches to اندر. بندگان is the plural noun “servants/attendants” and functions as the people located by the preceding spatial phrase.
segments.s697-g11: نشسته is a participle/adjective meaning “seated.” بران is the contracted poetic form of بر آن (“on that”), so بران تخت = “on that throne.” بی before a noun means “without,” and گفت‌وگو (here split as گفت + وگوی) is the compound noun “conversation” or “talk,” so بی گفت وگوی = “without conversation/in silence.”
segments.s697-g12: بگریان is a present‑participle/gerundial form meaning “weeping” or “in tears” and modifies زنی (“a woman”). ماند is the past tense verb “remained/was left” (third person singular classical usage), and آن خوب روی is a noun phrase “that fair‑faced (lit. good‑faced) one,” which identifies the woman. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: participle + noun, verb, then the identifying adjective phrase.
segments.s697-g13: زمان تا زمان is a repetitive temporal formula meaning “time after time” or “again and again.” دست is “hand,” and برآختی (poetic past) means “rose/was raised” or “lifted up.” Together دست برآختی describes raising the hand repeatedly; the verb is classical/poetic past.
segments.s697-g14: سرشکی (سرشک) is a small drop / teardrop. ز is the poetic preposition “from,” مژگان = “eyelashes,” and بینداختی is the past tense of انداختن with the prefix بیـ (here meaning ‘let fall’ or ‘cast down’): “you/ she let fall” (i.e., shed a tear). The whole phrase describes a tear dropping from the lashes.
segments.s697-g15: هرآنکس که is the relative “whoever/anyone who.” دیدی is the past tense “saw” (classical/poetic inflection). مر here is an older/poetic clitic meaning “to me” or marking the speaker’s perspective, and او را is the normal object phrase “her/him.” ز دور means “from a distance.” The phrase overall: “whoever saw her from afar (to my/one’s view)….”
segments.s697-g16: زنی یافتی is literally “you would find (her) a woman” or “(one) found (her) a woman” — یافتی is a past/factual verb used here in a generalizing sense. شیفته is an adjective meaning “enamored/ardent.” پر ز نور is the poetic order of modern پر از نور = “full of light” (پر = full; ز = of/from; نور = light). The adjectives follow the noun to describe the woman’s appearance.
segments.s697-g113: اگرخود is the conditional particle اگر plus the emphatic/reflexive خود ('if (you/he) yourself'). درین is a fused preposition+demonstrative (در + این) placed before کالبد ('in/into this body'). جان is the object 'life/soul' and بدی here is an archaic/epic verbal form meaning 'give' (in the conditional sense 'if (you) give').
segments.s697-g114: جز از introduces an exception 'except from'. دست جاییش is a genitive/possessive-like phrase 'the (place of) the hand' (دست = hand/power, جاییش = 'its place/position'). جنبان here describes the manner ('by the moving/movement of ...') and بدی repeats the same archaic verbal form 'to cause/give' so the clause reads 'it moves except from the movement of his hand'.
segments.s697-g115: سرشکش is a nominal phrase referring to the head/its motion (here 'he cast/turned his head'); سوی دیگر is a directional phrase 'to the other side'; انداختی is the epic past form (‑دی) meaning 'threw/turned/cast' (Ferdowsi uses -دی for past third-person). Word order is verb-final in the clause's old/epic style.
segments.s697-g116: وگر is the conditional 'and if' (or 'but if'). دست جای دگر is 'the hand to another place/position' (دگر = other/another). آختی is an archaic/poetic verb form here meaning roughly 'he moved/shifted' (the exact modern lemma isn't in the provided senses), so the clause means 'and if he moved his hand elsewhere'.
segments.s697-g117: نبینم is the negative present/prefixed form 'I do not see' (here a speaker's observation). همی is an emphatic/continuous particle ('ever/at all' or 'indeed') modifying جنبش 'movement'. جان و جسم are coordinated objects 'soul and body' (two linked nouns as the thing observed).
segments.s697-g118: نباشد is a negative present/subjunctive 'it is not / it cannot be'; جز از 'except from' introduces the lone possible cause; فیلسوفی here functions as a modifier of طلسم and means something like 'philosopher's / learned' (i.e. a learned man's spell) and طلسم is 'spell/enchantment'. Together the clause says 'it cannot be except (that it is) a philosopher's spell.'
segments.s697-g119: بر قیصر آمد is a directional phrase (بر = toward/upon) + proper name + آمد 'came' (past). In epic Persian the sequence بخندید و گفت uses two past verbs joined by و: 'he laughed and said' — بخندید is past third-person (‑دی) 'he laughed', وگفت is the connective 'and said'.
segments.s697-g120: که introduces reported speech 'that'. این ماه رخ را is a descriptive noun phrase 'this moon-faced one' (ماه used metaphorically for a beautiful face) with را marking the definite object/topic. خرد نیست جفت literally orders 'wisdom is not (a) match' — i.e. 'there is no match/equal in wisdom' — where خرد='wisdom', نیست='is not', and جفت='pair/match'.
segments.s697-g17: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that') modifying a preceding noun; بگریستی is a past-tense, third-person singular form meaning 'wept/cried' (classical poetic past with the b- prefix). بر + مسیحا is a prepositional phrase 'to/at the Messiah' with مسیحا in the vocative/addressing form; بزار is an imperative/optative 'let/allow' (an archaic poetic form), here representing the plea she utters. The clause therefore reports what she cried to the Messiah.
segments.s697-g18: Nominal descriptive phrase: دو رخ (literally 'two faces' = two cheeks) + adjective زرد ('pale/wan') — Persian often omits the copula, so this describes her appearance. و links the two images; مژگان (poetic plural of 'eyelashes') is compared with چو + ابر بهار ('like the cloud(s) of spring'), a simile introduced by چو. Note the noun–adjective order and the use of چو for likeness.
segments.s697-g19: طلسم بزرگان is a genitive construction 'the talisman/spell of the elders' (noun + genitive). چو آمد functions as a temporal clause 'when it came' (چو = 'when'); بجای here means 'into its place / in its stead' (broadly 'in position'). The whole segment sets the time/condition for what follows.
segments.s697-g20: Word order places the prepositional phrase بر قیصر ('to/onto Caesar') before the verb آمد ('came'), a common poetic inversion; یکی رهنمای is an indefinite nominal phrase 'a certain guide' or 'one guide' as the subject. Altogether: 'a guide came to Caesar.'
segments.s697-g21: ز دانا چو بشنید is a subordinate temporal/causal clause: ز (poetic 'from'/'on the report of') + دانا ('the wise one') + چو بشنید ('when he heard'). قیصر is the subject of the main clause and برفت is the past verb meaning 'went/took off (toward)'; in classical usage the b- prefix appears in past-tense verbs. (Note: the supplied senses list for برفت in the data appears unrelated to this verbal use.)
segments.s697-g22: به پیش طلسم آمد is literal 'he came into the presence of the talisman' (به پیش = 'to before/in the presence of', طلسم = 'talisman', آمد = 'came'). آنگاه is a temporal adverb 'then/afterwards'; تفت is a past verb here meaning something like 'examined/handled/interrogated' (an archaic/poetic verb form). The clause sequence describes Caesar's approach and immediate action.
segments.s697-g23: ازان ('from that' / 'because of that') + جادویی ('magic/enchantment') introduces the cause; در شگفتی بماند is a stative verbal clause 'remained in wonder/was astonished' (بماند = 'remained' in past/poetic form). The whole segment says he was left amazed by the magic.
segments.s697-g24: فرستاد ('he sent') is a simple past verb; و links the two actions. گستهم را پیش خواند is 'he summoned/called Gostaham before (him)' — را marks گستهم as the definite object, پیش = 'before/in the presence of', خواند here is 'to call/summon' (the third sense listed). Note: the provided sense for گستهم in the data does not match the use here (a personal name/object), so its sense index is given as unknown.
segments.s697-g25: بگستهم is the prepositional dative fused form (به + گستهم) meaning 'to Gostaham' — a prefixed be- attaches to the name. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' that follows the indirect object. ای is the vocative 'O!', and نامدار is an epithet 'renowned'; گو here appears in a vocative phrase and is ambiguous/poetic (parts are fused or elliptical in Ferdowsi). Word order: indirect object before verb, then the vocative address.
segments.s697-g26: This clause is the content of the speech. یکی دختری = 'one/a daughter' (یکی acts as an indefinite/article-like word). داشتم is first-person past 'I had' (the clausal subject 'I' is omitted). چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and نگار = 'beloved' (simile: 'like a beloved'). The direct object 'one daughter' precedes the verb as is common in Persian.
segments.s697-g27: ببالید is a past intransitive 'she grew/flourished'. و links clauses. آمدش = آمد + ـش (came to her) where the clitic -ش marks the experiencer. هنگام شوی = 'the time of becoming' (شوی from شدن 'to become') — here idiomatically 'the time of marriage/when she was to be married'. The whole phrase reads literally 'to her came the time of becoming (married)'.
segments.s697-g28: خیوی + ـش is compressed: یکی خویش = 'one (of) her habits/ways' (خوی = habit/disposition, with the possessive clitic -ش 'her'). بد qualifies خوی as 'bad habit'. مرو را = 'to/at Merv' (مرو the city, followed by the object marker را). نامجوی is a compound/old-fashioned form meaning 'one who seeks a name' or 'someone seeking a (good) name/reputation' — here contextually 'seeking a husband or repute' (the phrase as a whole: 'one bad habit of hers was that she would not go to Merv to seek [a name/suitor]').
segments.s697-g29: به راه مسیحا = a prepositional phrase 'into the way/way of the Messiah' (مسیحا = 'Messiah' with a poetic case ending). بدو = 'to him/to it' (dative clitic/adverbial), and دادمش is the speaker's past transitive 'I gave her' (دادم + ـش object clitic). Word order puts the prepositional phrase and dative before the verb 'gave'.
segments.s697-g30: ز = 'out of/because of' (poetic genitive/causal), بی‌دانشی = 'ignorance'. روی here is literal 'face' (and in context means to uncover or reveal the face), and بگشادمش is a first-person past 'I opened/revealed her' (ب‑ prefix + گشودم + ـش). The sequence says that, through ignorance, the speaker 'unveiled' her (literally 'opened her face').
segments.s697-g31: فرستادم is 'I sent' (past). او is the pronoun 'him/her'. رابخان is an archaic/fused sequence (را + بخوان or similar) — in context a fused object/construction meaning 'had him summoned' or 'sent to call' (the catalogued senses mark it as a fused 'ra+' construction). جوان qualifies the person as 'the young [man]'. The clause is compact/poetic, with fused elements that would be separate in modern prose.
segments.s697-g32: سوی آسمان = 'toward heaven'. شد is the past of شدن 'became' or an auxiliary here functioning like 'was set/made' — idiomatically 'was sent/was borne'. روان in this line is the noun 'soul/spirit' and جوان repeats 'the young [man]'; together they express 'the young man's spirit went up to heaven' (i.e. he died/was taken to heaven).
segments.s697-g81: This clause begins with ازیشان (colloquial/poetic contraction of از ایشان) meaning 'from them' or 'of them.' کسی appears with the negated verb ندید to yield the sense 'no one' (literal: 'from them anyone did not see'). روی پاسخ is a small prepositional/idiomatic phrase meaning 'in reply / as an answer' (روی = 'upon, with respect to' + پاسخ = 'reply'). ندید is the simple past of دیدن ('to see'); here the literal 'did not see' functions idiomatically to mean 'made no reply / did not answer.' Word order is slightly inverted (preposed adverbial/partitive + subject + object phrase + verb) as is common in poetry.
segments.s697-g82: زن بی‌زبان is a noun phrase 'the mute woman' (بی‌زبان = 'speechless, mute' modifying زن). خامشی is the direct object (or selected state) meaning 'silence'; placing the object before the verb is natural in Persian poetic style. برگزید is the simple past (3rd sing.) of برگزیدن 'to choose, select' and yields 'she chose.'
segments.s697-g83: ازان چاره is a frozen/poetic locution 'from that (state of) remedy/solution' (here: 'from that predicament / as a result of that situation'). نزدیک قیصر شدند: نزدیک ('near') + قیصر ('Caesar') forms a locative phrase 'near the Caesar,' followed by شدند (past pl. of شدن) meaning here 'they came/they drew near.' In older/poetic Persian شدن often functions as a full past copula/motion verb.
segments.s697-g84: ببیچارگی is an adverbial/nominal phrase built with the preposition/particle ب- + بیچارگی ('in wretchedness / in helplessness'), giving 'in their helpless state.' نزد here means 'in the presence of / before' (نزد داور = 'before the judge'). شدند again is the past plural 'they came / they presented themselves.' The clause as a whole describes their approaching the judge in a state of helplessness.
segments.s697-g85: که هرچند introduces a concessive subordinate clause ('that / although'). گفتیم و دادیم پند should be read as two coordinated past verbs 'گفتیم' ('we said') and 'و دادیم' ('and we gave') with پند ('advice') as their object: literally 'although we said and gave advice.' Note that in the printed word list ودادیم appears as a single token but here it functions as و + دادیم.
segments.s697-g86: نبد is an archaic/poetic negative copula meaning 'was not / did not prove to be.' The phrase پند ما places the possessive pronoun after the noun in older Persian order: 'our counsel.' مر ورا are dative/benefactive pronouns (vora = 'to him/her'); together they mark the recipient/beneficiary and here indicate that the advice was not useful to that person. سودمند is an adjective 'useful, profitable.' The whole clause: 'Our counsel was not useful to him.'
segments.s697-g87: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'so.' گفت is the simple past (3rd sing.) 'he said' (poetic word-listing of senses is irregular; here it is the verb 'said'); قیصر is the post-verbal subject (poetic inversion is common). The following که begins the reported/exclamatory content. بد روزگار is a frozen/binomial phrase meaning 'ill fate / bad times' (بد modifies روزگار).
segments.s697-g88: که introduces the content of Caesar's speech. ما سوکواریم is a present/state expression 'we are sukwār' (سوکوار = 'mounted archer/horseman'; the -یم ending marks 1st person plural). زین سوکوار is an attributive phrase 'this mounted archer' (زین here is the demonstrative/attributive usage tied to the following noun or 'on/with this saddle' in a literal sense). The clause is elliptical/condensed (poetic): Caesar laments their ill fortune that they, though horsemen/archers themselves, must contend with this particular mounted man.

sentences.s697-s49: When Khurad mounted the saddle, he fixed his gaze on him — (looking at) his face, head, and helmet.
sentences.s697-s50: For a long time he remained before her; a deceptive spell had taken away his devotion/prayer.
sentences.s697-s51: He said much, and the woman gave no answer; the man of distinguished lineage became thoughtful.
sentences.s697-s52: He looked the woman over from head to foot; he saw worshippers around her.
sentences.s697-s25: At first the brave man came in to ponder the words, thinking that evil would be to his advantage.
sentences.s697-s26: He said to him, 'O daughter of Caesar's line — may the wise not be estranged from the cause of justice.'
sentences.s697-s27: There is no escape from the death-dealing winged eagle, whether the lion in the thicket or the fish in the water.
sentences.s697-s28: May everyone speak ill of the champion, for the woman was mute and the body lifeless.
sentences.s697-s1: When the circling sun grew pale, the star entered the nightingale's constellation.
sentences.s697-s2: Caesar ordered the trick-maker to devise far-reaching schemes.
sentences.s697-s3: Make a wondrous talisman so that no one will recognize him by his body.
sentences.s697-s4: A fair woman sat on a delicate throne of coquettish luxury, full of modesty and clad in finely fashioned garments.
sentences.s697-s17: Now he sits in mourning and sorrow; the day has become bright as lapis-lazuli.
sentences.s697-s18: He accepts neither my counsel nor utters a word; the new world, because of his suffering, has become old.
sentences.s697-s19: Let someone bear the suffering and look at him; choose the words of the wise.
sentences.s697-s20: Youth and the hero's mettle — unless he speaks with you, his tongue will not open.
sentences.s697-s61: It is a talisman that these Romans made, by which they did not recognize Baluy and Gostaham.
sentences.s697-s62: If you smile upon the Iranians, or if you were to shut our eyes.
sentences.s697-s63: When he hears this, the king grows smiling; his face opens and his teeth gleam like silver.
sentences.s697-s21: He said to him, "What shall I do, O schemer, except try to drive the pain out of his heart?"
sentences.s697-s22: That famed one came up to the talisman, open‑hearted and eager for speech.
sentences.s697-s23: When he came near his high throne, the talisman took the prayer from upon the throne.
sentences.s697-s24: The noble Gostaham sat down humbled, and spoke with the archer’s maiden.
sentences.s697-s45: When he found no remedy among those renowned men, he hastened to the counsel of wisdom and sprang onto the saddle.
sentences.s697-s46: He said to him, 'O renowned scribe, choose the chief of Ardashir's seed.'
sentences.s697-s47: Send someone to this maiden, so that you may hear her voice at least once.
sentences.s697-s48: He sent with him a stalwart man from the iwan to the vicinity of that archer.
sentences.s697-s29: She would, at every moment, let a tear fall onto her finger before the eloquent physician.
sentences.s697-s30: When he was astonished by her and remained in wonder, Caesar sent someone to summon her.
sentences.s697-s31: He said to him, "What did you see concerning my daughter — that from her my crown will be darkened?"
sentences.s697-s32: He said to him, 'I gave her much advice, but my counsel did not take effect on her.'
sentences.s697-s53: She kept saying, “If the woman is sorrowful for him, why is the worshipper sometimes so silent?”
sentences.s697-s54: If he himself is humbled in her sight, then it would be fitting if his anger were diminished.
sentences.s697-s55: He kept letting it fall before her, and he could not move either to the left or to the right.
sentences.s697-s56: When the little tear fell, it went to one place; neither his hand moved nor his foot.
sentences.s697-s33: The next day the Caesar said to Baluy, “Today pair up with Andiyan.”
sentences.s697-s34: Likewise Shapur, foremost of noble birth, made our hearts glad by giving the maiden Râbedin.
sentences.s697-s35: Become, before this maiden, a marksman; speak of the illustrious sovereign.
sentences.s697-s36: Unless you obtain an answer from my daughter by which fire would come upon my head.
sentences.s697-s37: Perhaps she will hear your advice and counsel and so learn your capital and worth.
sentences.s697-s38: I am resolved that today she will answer; and when she answers, she will give it in a joyful voice.
sentences.s697-s39: The archer will be freed from this sorrow, so that bloody water will pour beside (the shore).
sentences.s697-s40: Those three honored free-men departed; a spokesman spoke, and not one of them suffered disgrace.
sentences.s697-s5: Worshippers came from this side and that, and servants stood before and behind him.
sentences.s697-s6: Seated on that throne, without conversation, that fair‑faced woman remained weeping.
sentences.s697-s7: Time after time she lifted her hand; she let a teardrop fall from her eyelashes.
sentences.s697-s8: Whoever saw her from afar would find the woman enamored, full of light.
sentences.s697-s57: If you yourself put life into this body, it will move only by a movement of his hand.
sentences.s697-s58: He turned his head to the other side, and if he shifted his hand to another place...
sentences.s697-s59: I see no movement of soul or body; it must be nothing but a philosopher's spell.
sentences.s697-s60: He came up to Caesar, laughed, and said that this moon-faced one has no match in wisdom.
sentences.s697-s9: who wept and cried to the Messiah, "Allow it"; her two cheeks were pale and her eyelashes like the clouds of spring.
sentences.s697-s10: When the elders' talisman came into its place, a guide came to Caesar.
sentences.s697-s11: When he heard from the wise man, Caesar went; he came before the talisman, and then examined it.
sentences.s697-s12: He remained astonished by that magic; he sent (someone) and summoned Gostaham before him.
sentences.s697-s13: He said to Gostaham, “O renowned one, I once had a daughter like a beloved.”
sentences.s697-s14: She grew up, and when the time for marriage came to her, one of her bad habits was that she would not go to Merv to seek a name (or suitor).
sentences.s697-s15: I gave her to the path of the Messiah; out of ignorance I uncovered (revealed/unveiled) her face.
sentences.s697-s16: I sent him (I caused the young man to be summoned); the young man's spirit was borne toward heaven.
sentences.s697-s41: None of them answered; the mute woman chose silence.
sentences.s697-s42: From that predicament they drew near the Caesar; in their helplessness they presented themselves before the judge.
sentences.s697-s43: Although we spoke and gave counsel, our advice did not prove useful to him.
sentences.s697-s44: Thus Caesar said: 'What ill fortune — that we, who are horsemen/archers, should face this mounted archer!'
